data.title: Tours to visit Christmas movie locations in US state of Connecticut prove huge hit
data.description:
Christmas at Pemberley Manor and Romance at Reindeer Lodge may never make it to Oscar night, but legions of fans still love these sweet-yet-predictable holiday movies – and this season, many are making pilgrimages to where their favourite scenes were filmed.
That is because the US state of Connecticut – the location for at least 22 holiday films by American cable networks such as Hallmark and Lifetime – has been promoting tours of the quaint Christmas-card cities and towns featured in this booming film market. Places where, for example, a busy corporate lawyer can return home for the holidays and cross paths with a plaid-shirt-clad former high school flame who now runs a Christmas tree farm. (Spoiler alert: they live happily ever after.)
“It’s exciting – just to know that something was in a movie and we actually get to see it visually,” said Abby Rumfelt of Morganton, North Carolina, after stepping off a coach in Wethersfield, Connecticut, one of the stops on the holiday film tour.
Rumfelt was among 53 people, mostly women, on a recent week-long “Hallmark Movie Christmas Tour”, organised by Mayfield Tours from Spartanburg, South Carolina. On the bus, fans watched the matching film as they rode from stop to stop.

Whether you love them or loathe them, television Christmas films are a holiday season staple. The romance, the snowy landscapes, the perfectly decorated homes and the familiar stories provide millions of viewers with comfort and joy in December – and even November for some with a looser definition of the holiday season.
Of course, some of that comforting familiarity comes from the conventions that are now expected in a network Christmas film – for example, the idea of reconnecting with a school sweetheart or the presence of an older, bearded gentleman who may or may not be Santa Claus.
Hallmark holiday films have reached 24 million people so far this season and Lifetime saw more than 23 million people tune in last year, according to data from Nielsen.
It is no surprise these tours have been a hit. To plan one, Mayfield Tours co-owner Debbie Mayfield used the “Connecticut Christmas Movie Trail” map, which was launched by the New England state last year to cash in on the growing Christmas film craze.
Mayfield, who co-owns the company with her husband, Ken, said this was their first Christmas tour to holiday film locations in Connecticut and other Northeastern states. It included hotel accommodation, some meals, tickets and even a stop to see the Rockettes in New York City. It sold out in two weeks.
With snow in the air and Christmas songs piped from a speaker, the group stopped for lunch at Heirloom Market at Comstock Ferre, where parts of the Hallmark films Christmas on Honeysuckle Lane and Rediscovering Christmas were filmed.

Once home to America’s oldest seed company, the store is located in a historical district known for its stately 1700s and 1800s buildings. It is an ideal setting for a holiday film. Even the local country store has sold T-shirts featuring Hallmark’s crown logo and the phrase “I Live in a Christmas Movie. Wethersfield, CT 06109.”
“People just know about us now,” said Julia Koulouris, who co-owns the market with her husband, Spiros, crediting the film trail in part. “And you see these things on Instagram and stuff where people are tagging it and posting it.”
The concept of holiday films dates back to the 1940s, when Hollywood produced classics like It’s A Wonderful Life, Miracle on 34th Street and Christmas in Connecticut, which was actually shot at the Warner Bros studios in Burbank, California.
In 2006, five years after the launch of the Hallmark Channel on TV, Hallmark “struck gold” with the romance film The Christmas Card, said Joanna Wilson, author of the book Tis the Season TV: The Encyclopedia of Christmas-Themed Episodes, Specials and Made-for-TV Movies.
“Hallmark saw those high ratings and then started creating that format and that formula with the tropes and it now has become their dominant formula that they create for their Christmas TV romances,” she said.

The holiday film industry, estimated to generate hundreds of millions of dollars a year, has expanded beyond Hallmark and Lifetime.
Today, a mix of cable and broadcast networks, streaming platforms and direct-to-video producers release roughly 100 new films annually, Wilson said. The genre has also diversified, with characters from a wider range of racial and ethnic backgrounds as well as LGBTQ storylines.
The formula, however, remains the same. And fans still have an appetite for a family-friendly love story.
“They want to see people coming together. They want to see these romances. It’s a part of the hope of the season,” Wilson said. “Who doesn’t love love? And it always has a predictable, happy ending.”
Hazel Duncan, 83, of Forest City, North Carolina, said she and her husband of 65 years, Owen, like to watch the films together year-round because they are sweet and family-friendly. They also take her back to their early years as a young couple, when life felt simpler.
“We hold hands sometimes,” she said. “It’s kind of sweet. We’ve got two recliners back in a bedroom that’s real small and we’ve got the TV there. And we close the doors off and it’s just our time together in the evening.”

Connecticut’s chief marketing officer, Anthony M. Anthony, said the Christmas Movie Trail is part of a multipronged rebranding effort launched in 2023 that promotes the state not just as a tourist destination, but also as a place to work and live.
“So what better way to highlight our communities as a place to call home than them being sets of movies?” he said.
However, there continues to be debate at the state Capitol over whether to eliminate or cap film industry tax credits – which could threaten how many more of these films will be made locally.
Christina Nieves and her husband of 30 years, Raul, already live in Connecticut and have been tackling the trail “little by little”.
It had been a chance, she said, to explore new places in the state, like the Bushnell Park Carousel in Hartford, where a scene from Ghost of Christmas Always was filmed.

It also inspired Nieves to convince her husband – not quite the film fan she is – to join her at a tree lighting and Christmas parade in their hometown of Windsor Locks.
“I said, listen, let me just milk this Hallmark thing as long as I can, OK?” she said.
Additional reporting by Tribune News Service

data.title: Ben Ainslie strikes Oakley Capital deal to keep British America’s Cup bid afloat
data.description:
British ‌sailor Ben Ainslie has finally secured the backing he needs for his 2027 America’s Cup campaign, announcing a deal ‍with Oakley Capital on Tuesday after what he admitted has been a stressful chapter in his mission to “bring the Cup home”.
Ainslie’s Athena Racing team said a majority ownership investment from the British private equity group would provide “long-term financial stability to compete not only in the next America’s Cup but across multiple future cycles”.
“Up ⁠until now, basically, I’ve been funding the team myself … yeah, I’d say it’s been pretty stressful. But I believed in the team, I believed in the [America’s Cup] partnership, and I was willing to take that risk. So I’m delighted that that’s been worthwhile,” Ainslie said.
As well as being the most successful Olympic sailor, the 48-year-old also lifted the “Auld Mug” in 2013 with Oracle Team USA and has made it his ‍goal to secure a first victory for Britain, where the event was first contested off the south coast in 1851.
However, Ainslie was left ‍without funding for his latest campaign to win the coveted trophy after parting ways in January with INEOS, which had financed his attempts in 2021 and 2024.

Athena Racing said Oakley will help them to take ‌full advantage of the newly formed America’s Cup Partnership (ACP), which has been backed by five teams and will mean the event is now held every ‍two years, raising its visibility for sponsors.
“The America’s Cup is the pinnacle of sailing and an icon of sport, won by only four nations since 1851,” Peter Dubens, Oakley’s co-founder and managing partner, said.
“With strong governance and a neutral management team focused on audience and media growth, the competition is set to broaden its appeal, improve accessibility and ‌ensure long-term ⁠sustainability.”
Ainslie, who will remain team principal, said Oakley’s investment would give Britain all the financial firepower needed to challenge defenders New Zealand in Naples, Italy, in 2027.
The new ACP framework imposes a spending cap of €75 million (US$88 million) for each team’s AC38 campaign, then €55 million for subsequent events.
While the Oakley deal opened the door to sponsorship, Ainslie said he did ‌not expect to announce anything imminently.
“We’ve been having some positive conversations with various brands … The agencies that we’ve been talking to that are supporting us with the sponsorship search are really bullish and positive about what we’re selling,” Ainslie added.
The British ‌team will get a rebrand and ‌the boat they used to challenge New Zealand last year is already in transit from Barcelona to Naples where it will undergo modifications.
Ainslie said the team and their boat would have a very British feel and focus, adding that having this association with the country is an important feature for the America’s Cup.
“We are really trying to come up with something innovative and exciting for all the supporters out there,” he said.

data.title: Alibaba’s DingTalk rolls out Agent OS as it bets bigger on AI agents
data.description:
DingTalk, the workplace collaboration app developed by Alibaba Group Holding, has unveiled a dedicated operating system and new enterprise hardware as it steps up its push into AI agents amid an intensifying arms race among China’s tech giants.
At an event in Hangzhou on Tuesday, the Alibaba unit launched Agent OS, an operating system designed specifically for AI agents, alongside DingTalk Real, a hardware suite that serves as a physical terminal for running those agents. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
“Moving forward, all AI agents on DingTalk will be built and run on Agent OS, allowing AI to connect directly with the physical world,” Chen Hang, founder and chief executive of DingTalk, said at the event.
AI agents – such as those developed by Chinese start-up Butterfly Effect’s Manus AI – are software programmes capable of autonomously planning and executing tasks on behalf of users or systems, breaking down complex goals into specific actions using available resources.
The move underscores DingTalk’s ambition to evolve from a pure software platform into a full-stack provider of AI agent technologies, combining both hardware and software for enterprise customers.
The push comes as Alibaba competes more aggressively with domestic rivals including Tencent Holdings and ByteDance, which are also accelerating their investments in AI agents.

Chinese Big Tech firms have sharpened their focus on the segment following the overseas relocation of Manus AI, a high-profile AI agent project founded by Chinese entrepreneurs, amid rising geopolitical tensions.
Chen described DingTalk Real as the “body of AI”, designed to enable agents to operate “securely and reliably” within complex corporate environments.
He emphasised the company’s focus on safety, noting that the hardware allowed users to halt operations by physically cutting power if an AI agent behaves unpredictably.
DingTalk also introduced a specialised “homework correction machine” aimed at educational use, capable of grading up to 100 assignments in four minutes. Targeting primary and middle schools, the device is available for pre-orders, with an initial roll-out planned for 1,000 schools.
On the software side, DingTalk rolled out a suite of workplace AI agents that automate tasks such as recruitment, travel expense claims and marketing design.
The AI Travel agent, for instance, could plan itineraries, compare prices and file expense claim requests automatically on behalf of users, according to Chen.
Chen founded DingTalk in 2015 before leaving Alibaba six years later to pursue his own venture. He returned earlier this year as chief executive, as Alibaba ramped up its AI strategy across its broader ecosystem.

data.title: Taiwan ramps up security measures for coming holidays after mass stabbing
data.description:
Taiwan has bolstered security ahead of the coming holidays to pre-empt potential copycat attacks following a deadly mass stabbing that has left the island on edge.
The island’s Ministry of the Interior and National Police Agency (NPA) ordered tighter deployments at transport hubs and major events, increased monitoring of online threats and accelerated reviews of police resources and command procedures.
Announced in separate statements on Tuesday, the measures came after last week’s violence triggered a surge of public anxiety and a wave of threatening and copycat posts on social media.
The attacks unfolded on Friday afternoon in central Taipei, when a 27-year-old man identified by authorities as Chang Wen launched assaults in and around Taipei Main Station and the nearby Zhongshan commercial district.
Police said Chang began his rampage at about 3.40pm by setting fires along roads, damaging cars and motorbikes, before torching the flat where he lived. About an hour later, he used smoke devices to create confusion near two exits of Taipei Main Station and carried out knife attacks in crowded public areas inside the station and a nearby department store.


Three people were killed and 11 injured, police said. Chang later died after falling from a building. Prosecutors said the motive behind his indiscriminate violence was still under investigation.
According to police, Chang had previously volunteered in an air force communications unit but was discharged after a drink-driving incident. He had been wanted since July for failing to report for short-term reserve military service, they added.
Investigators said searches of Chang’s rented flat and a nearby hotel showed he had been planning the attacks for more than a year and a half.
Killings are rare in Taiwan, which is widely regarded as a safe society. Friday’s attacks prompted justice authorities to order the establishment of a supervisory emergency response task force.
All local prosecutors’ offices have been instructed to immediately set up “anti-terrorism emergency response teams”.
In a statement, the justice ministry said: “Through cross-agency coordination and information integration, authorities will comprehensively strengthen early warning, reporting and rapid response mechanisms to ensure threats are detected early and handled swiftly.”
With Christmas, New Year and pre-Lunar New Year events expected to draw large crowds across Taiwan, the ministry also instructed prosecutors and investigators to actively take part in security coordination meetings for major public activities.
According to the ministry, such efforts should include “enforcing facility safety inspections and working with multiple agencies to build a layered security network to ensure public safety and prevent institutional security vulnerabilities”.
Authorities have also moved swiftly to clamp down on copycat threats and online intimidation. The ministry said prosecutors and investigators had been ordered to act quickly against posts that appeared to threaten attacks or encourage violence.

Since Friday’s attacks, police have traced multiple alleged threats, some originating from overseas IP addresses, and warned that spreading messages that endanger public safety could carry criminal penalties.
Several people have been arrested after posting online claims that they were associates of the suspect and would continue attacks at metro and train stations in southern, central and northern Taiwan, officers said.
Meanwhile, police have urged the public not to amplify rumours or speculation, after false claims circulated suggesting the case was linked to foreign forces or a so-called fifth column. Authorities have called the assertions unfounded and said they risked inflaming public panic.
As part of the holiday security push, the NPA said police had prioritised high-visibility patrols and layered security at venues, shopping districts and public transport stations island-wide.
The agency said it had identified 137 major events scheduled between December 21 and 31, including road races, concerts and seasonal festivals, and ordered local police departments to strengthen duty rosters and security planning.
In total, 17,295 police and civilian personnel would be deployed, the interior ministry said, including nearly 12,000 police officers, with the rest made up of civil defence members, neighbourhood watch volunteers, private security staff and volunteer police and traffic officers.
Authorities have also signalled tougher scrutiny of items used to create disruption.
Chang Jung-hsin, the NPA’s director general, told lawmakers on Monday that officials were considering regulating certain smoke devices and flares “at the source”. This may include a real-name registration system, with an internal review expected within a month.
Beyond immediate security measures, the incident has triggered a broader debate over whether Taiwan’s current policing model is capable of handling fast-moving violence that crosses districts and combines arson, smoke devices and bladed attacks.


During parliamentary questioning on Monday, ruling Democratic Progressive Party lawmaker Huang Jie urged the public to focus on systemic shortcomings rather than blaming frontline officers.
“The case exposed the need to strengthen cross-jurisdictional reporting and command mechanisms,” she said.
Huang also highlighted staffing constraints in the Taipei Metro network, noting that the system had 117 stations but only 168 metro police assigned to field duties, leaving fewer than 80 officers on duty at any given time after shift rotations.
Equipment shortages have also emerged as a flashpoint.
“Some officers had complained that issued gear was insufficient or of poor quality, forcing them to buy items such as pepper spray, body-worn cameras, batons and even ballistic vests at their own expense,” the lawmaker said.
The NPA has pledged to review equipment distribution and procurement to ensure frontline officers have adequate and reliable gear.
Some public safety commentators have used the recent attacks to argue for technology upgrades to improve early detection and response.
Among them is Chang Ching-sen, a former minister without portfolio, who argued in a social media post that “Taiwan has been peaceful for too long, leading to complacency in maintaining public safety”.
The former minister said that while transport hubs and large public spaces were equipped with numerous cameras, most were using passive recording systems, which he said were useful for investigations after the fact but offered limited value in emergencies.
“Without intelligent analysis and real-time integration, potentially dangerous behaviour may not be detected and stopped quickly enough,” he added, stating that regular drills were also needed to ensure that unusual incidents could be handled within minutes.

data.title: Rare Rafflesia flower rediscovery in Indonesia revives push to drop colonial name
data.description:
A giant flower’s rediscovery in a West Sumatran forest of Indonesia has spurred a growing campaign among researchers to change the colonial-era name of one of the world’s most famous plants.
Last month’s find of an extremely rare Rafflesia hasseltii – one of the giant, foul-smelling blooms often dubbed “corpse flowers” – has energised researchers and activists seeking to strip the genus of its association with Thomas Stamford Raffles, a figure widely credited with founding colonial Singapore but criticised by scholars and campaigners for violence and coercion during Britain’s rule in parts of Southeast Asia.
Recognised as among the world’s largest flowers, the Rafflesia entered Western scientific records in 1818, when a local guide led British naturalist Joseph Arnold to the plant in what is now Bengkulu, on Sumatra’s west coast.
The genus was later named after Raffles, then the province’s lieutenant governor, while the species Rafflesia arnoldii honoured Arnold.

West Sumatra’s recently rediscovered Rafflesia hasseltii bears the name of Dutch botanist Arend Ludolf van Hasselt.
The genus name “Rafflesia” is now deeply embedded in global science, covering about 42 known species found across Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand and the Philippines.
Within Indonesia, however, the flower is known by many different names depending on region, language and species, said Joko Witono. His team at the National Research and Innovation Agency, working with a biologist from Oxford University, observed the recent hasseltii bloom, which later went viral online.
The hasseltii is commonly known as cendawan muka rimau in West Sumatra, he told The Jakarta Post.
Environmental historian Luthfi Adam, a scholar of Dutch colonial science, said that species were generally named after the financial sponsors of the expeditions that “discovered” them.
“It may help identify the species more precisely,” Luthfi said. “But consequently, it will remove the local context and knowledge about the species.”
Calls to rethink that legacy have been taken up by Lennard Gillman, an ecology and biogeography professor at Auckland University of Technology, who has urged scientists to reconsider honouring figures such as Raffles, whom critics accuse of brutality during Britain’s colonial rule in parts of Southeast Asia.
“Essentially, like the plant he’s named on, he was a stinker,” Gillman said. “I don’t think that the scientific community should be honouring people like that with names on species at all.”
Indigenous communities had established names for plants and animals centuries before European scientists recorded them, and sidelining those names could undermine conservation by weakening local connections to biodiversity, Gillman added.
He also rejected arguments from some scientists that renaming would “destabilise” biological classification.
“Names change all the time. There’s already a lot of name changing in the system, and restoring some indigenous names would be minor by comparison,” Gillman said.
Even so, he acknowledged that altering scientific names is slow and contentious, requiring international agreement under formal rules of botanical nomenclature.

At last year’s International Botanical Congress in Spain, more than 100 scientists spent six days voting on a proposal to replace the names of more than 200 plant, fungal and algal species considered offensive or inappropriate.
“Finding a name that honours everyone would be difficult. But that doesn’t mean the conversation shouldn’t happen,” Gillman said.
The hasseltii’s rediscovery also sparked debate about academic colonialism in Indonesia after Oxford University initially credited only its own scientist in a social media post, omitting Indonesian researchers who took part in the fieldwork.
Following public criticism, the university amended its statement to include the full team.

data.title: Economic ‘pragmatism’ powers Indonesians’ positive view of China, survey finds
data.description:
China has emerged as a broadly positive partner for Indonesia in the public mind, according to a new survey, driven by visible economic engagement and investment on the ground, even as public support stops short of seeing Beijing as a full strategic partner.
The 2025 China–Indonesia Survey, released on December 18 by Jakarta-based think tank Centre of Economic and Law Studies (Celios), suggests Indonesians increasingly view ties with Beijing through a transactional lens, welcoming economic and technological cooperation while remaining cautious in other areas.
Overall, 66 per cent of the 1,022 respondents surveyed described relations between Jakarta and Beijing as positive, particularly in the areas of economic and technological cooperation.
President Prabowo Subianto was seen as “close” to Beijing by 94 per cent of respondents, a relationship they said could generate economic opportunities, strengthen Indonesia’s diplomacy and help balance US influence.
Mainland China was Indonesia’s third-largest foreign investor with US$5.4 billion in realised investment between January and September this year, behind Singapore’s US$12.6 billion and Hong Kong’s US$7.3 billion, according to the Indonesia Investment Coordinating Board.

Most respondents, or 78 per cent, supported Prabowo’s plan to extend the China-funded Jakarta-Bandung high-speed railway to Surabaya, citing transport modernisation, improved connectivity, and supporting economic growth as reasons behind their support.
China is also the favourite destination for overseas study for 31 per cent of respondents, beating Australia, Britain, the European Union and the United States.
When it comes to trade, only 32 per cent of respondents preferred China to be Indonesia’s top partner, while 22 per cent chose Western states and 17 per cent opted for Southeast Asian neighbours.
“Overall, the survey mirrors public hope that the Indonesia-China relationship can yield positive results,” Muhammad Zulfikar Rakhmat, director of the China-Indonesia desk at Celios, told This Week in Asia.
According to Muhammad, the positive public perception of China is due to its “visible” investment activities in Indonesia.
“In general, the relations [between Indonesia and China] have been transactional and conditional,” he said.
“In the future, as long as [China] delivers, the relationship will go further. The Indonesian people assume that there are many benefits that can be obtained from this relationship because there is tangibility; the people can see [China-funded] projects.”
Yeta Purnama, a Celios researcher focusing on China-Indonesia relations, said Beijing’s “soft diplomacy” had helped shape the public’s view of bilateral ties.
“[China] is very active, especially in the education sector. There have been many collaborations, visits, and other activities taking place at various campuses in Indonesia. In addition, sister city cooperation in various regions is also carried out very actively. This form of soft diplomacy appears to be quite successful in influencing public perception,” she said.


Lagging in defence
The survey underlines that “pragmatism” is the foundation of the Indonesian public’s positive views of China, especially on economic cooperation, according to Yeremia Lalisang, an international relations lecturer at the University of Indonesia.
“When asked about their preferred primary partner, only around 30 per cent of respondents named China. Pragmatism doesn’t really translate into a perception that China is our main trade partner. The [survey] indicates that respondents support partner diversification in Indonesia’s foreign relations,” said Yeremia, who was not involved in the survey.
China has established itself as Indonesia’s biggest trade partner, with bilateral trade reaching US$135.17 billion in 2024.
In defence, 42 per cent of respondents voted Russia as the top source of defence and military equipment, followed by China’s 19 per cent, and the US with 13 per cent.
“This shows that the comprehensive strategic partnership between Indonesia and China has not yet gone beyond economic partnership,” Yeremia said.
Respondents might also have been influenced by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and its role in the civil war in Syria, Yeta said.
“For Russia, war is seen as a showcase of military power. China has also seen its military strength increase in recent years. Meanwhile, the involvement of Western countries in financing arms for Israel has influenced public perception in Indonesia, which is generally on the side of Palestine,” Yeta said.

Points of friction
While Jakarta generally enjoys smooth relations with Beijing, analysts point out that some areas of friction remain, particularly in sovereignty issues.
The survey found that the 34 per cent of respondents who viewed China negatively were driven by concerns relating to maritime boundaries in the South China Sea, lack of trust, negative media coverage of China, and human rights issues.
Indonesia is not a claimant in the South China Sea territorial dispute, but part of its resource-rich North Natuna Sea falls within China’s so-called nine-dash lines, under which Beijing lays claim to most of the disputed waterway.
Recent negative media coverage has also centred on the existence of an airport inside a nickel hub – dotted with Chinese companies – in Central Sulawesi. The airport was accused of operating international flights without proper authorisation and dubbed by officials as “a state within a state”.
Yeta said the Indonesian public was also concerned about Chinese exports flooding local markets and potentially threatening the country’s businesses, including big manufacturing companies.
Despite the friction, Jakarta and Beijing are expected to forge closer ties next year, particularly in trade and investment, even as analysts urge Prabowo to keep diversifying export markets and boost ties with other nations.
“China will always be part of our calculations. In the future, it is difficult to imagine Indonesia’s foreign relations moving significantly away from China, but China can never be the only partner,” Yeremia said.

data.title: Judges slam lawyers, quash convictions of 11 Hong Kong villagers in ‘ding’ case
data.description:
A Hong Kong appellate court has quashed the convictions of 11 men who were found guilty of conspiring to fraudulently obtain building licences from the government more than a decade ago, finding that they did not receive a fair trial because of their lawyers’ incompetence.
In a written judgment delivered on Tuesday, the Court of Appeal ruled that the appellants’ lawyers failed to act in their clients’ best interests by adopting a defence strategy that best suited a developer prosecuted in the same case and a “wider circle” that was concerned with the outcome of the landmark trial in 2015.
“We feel compelled to find [the appellants] have been deprived of a fair trial in what is arguably the worst case of conflict that this court, in its collective experience, has been asked to consider,” the three presiding judges said in their verdict.
The appeal arose from the first criminal trial implicating those involved in the sales and purchases of the so-called ding right, which allows male indigenous villagers to seek permission to build three-storey homes in the New Territories.
In 2015, the District Court handed down jail sentences of up to three years to developer David Lee Yam-pui and 11 men from Sha Tin’s Tai Che village for conspiring to deceive the government into believing the villagers intended to build houses for their own occupation between 2008 and 2011.
While Lee appeared to have sold certain plots to the 11 residents, he had covertly acquired their ding right and had them forfeit their interests in the land once they successfully applied for a building licence from the Lands Department.
Lee’s family dropped his appeal after he died in 2022.
Defence counsel Philip Wong Chi-wai, who represented all 12 accused in the trial, advanced what he called a “broad and general defence” – that all the villagers involved made true declarations to the department when applying for a licence, and it was impossible to infer dishonesty on their part.
Justices Carlye Chu Fun-ling, Derek Pang Wai-cheong and Anthea Pang Po-kam found that Wong failed to consider alternative strategies for the 11 villagers and properly advise them to testify in court regarding what they believed was the legality of their actions.
The appeal proceedings revealed that Wong’s representation was the result of a discussion between his instructing lawyer, Jim Yiu-ming, and the Heung Yee Kuk, the rural powerhouse representing the interests of landlords.
The appellants’ legal fees were settled by an anonymous person who happened to have redeemed the mortgage that Lee took out for the development project in the village.

None of the 11 could contact their legal representatives without the help of a middleman identified as Cheng Ching-on, who also had a financial interest in the scheme.
The appellants said they were told to sign documents purporting to endorse certain legal instructions without knowing what they were. Some also complained they had never met the lawyers individually and were discouraged from giving evidence in the trial.
The judges said the events were disturbing and made one wonder if the “atrocious” idea was true that the original defence team intended to dissuade the 11 appellants from taking the witness box because what they said might harm Lee’s case.
The court found it impossible to dismiss the complaint of conflict of interest against Wong and Jim, even as it brushed aside the appellants’ constitutional challenge to the rural housing policy.
In 2021, the city’s top court upheld male indigenous villagers’ right to apply to build three-storey houses on their ancestral or government-granted land, although the department still retains the discretion to grant or dismiss an application.
The Court of Appeal ruled on Tuesday that the government’s requirement for applicants to declare their eligibility under the policy was justified to prevent abuse of the mechanism.
Prosecutors have not indicated whether they will seek a retrial for the 11 appellants.

data.title: ‘Saturation attacks’: drone swarms key for wartime victory, Chinese military outlet says
data.description:
The key to achieving wartime victory through expendable drones lies in “saturation attacks based on numerical superiority”, according to the Chinese military’s mouthpiece as the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) accelerates its development of unmanned systems.
The PLA Daily published a commentary on Tuesday titled “Exploring the Winning Formula for Unmanned Attrition Warfare” written by Zhou Xiaoli, Zhang Changfang and Zhu Qichao, although the outlet did not specify their institutional affiliations.
The authors said that in future warfare, it would be imperative to “flexibly deploy low-cost unmanned systems to continuously deplete the opponent’s high-value combat assets”, thereby “achieving optimal combat effectiveness with minimal battlefield expenditure”.


The article said that by releasing large numbers of drones in concentrated formations, unmanned systems could “launch simultaneous operations across multiple domains and directions”, thereby “forcing the enemy into a state of exhaustion and passive defence”.
Furthermore, unmanned combat forces could coordinate across multiple battlefield sectors, the article said. For instance, ground unmanned combat vehicles and aerial drones could operate in tandem, with “the former responsible for firepower strikes and target guidance, while the latter handles reconnaissance and support”.
And in wartime practice, unmanned combat systems could induce exhaustion in adversaries through “routine, incremental and sustained operations”.
Moreover, leveraging the flexibility, intelligence and diversity of unmanned systems could better achieve “asymmetric victory”, according to the article.
To improve the effectiveness of unmanned combat, such systems required greater autonomy, coordination and intelligence while also “integrating traditional tactics with unmanned technology”.
The PLA Daily article was published as Beijing assertively develops unmanned technology, viewing it as having a pivotal role in modern warfare and asymmetric combat as its operational capabilities are on display on the battlefield in Ukraine.
Drone operations are also regarded as a key factor in potential regional conflicts, including those in the Taiwan Strait region.
On December 11, the state news agency Xinhua reported that China’s massive aerial Jiu Tian drone carrier had completed its first flight, a debut that signalled the PLA’s potential swarming capacities, but the report did not mention when this occurred.
The Jiu Tian made its debut at China’s premier aviation showcase, the Zhuhai air show, in November last year. It is reported to carry up to 100 cruise weapons or small unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), including suicide drones. These armaments can be deployed simultaneously from both sides of the fuselage, significantly extending its operational range.
It can also carry a range of payloads on eight hardpoints and is capable of intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance assignments, as well as electronic warfare. Military commentators have noted that these characteristics will give the Jiu Tian exceptional saturation strike capabilities.
Days later, state broadcaster CCTV reported that China’s latest long-endurance stealth UAV, the CH-7, had also completed its maiden flight.
The drone is capable of long endurance, stealth and high cruising speed, ground reconnaissance, data support and communications under complex conditions, according to CCTV.
In September, China showcased multiple new unmanned systems during its Victory Day parade, including loyal wingman drones, collaborative combat aircraft and unmanned combat vehicles.


Meanwhile, China’s primary geopolitical rival, the United States, is also seeking to establish its advantage on the unmanned battlefield.
In June, US President Donald Trump signed multiple executive orders aimed at bolstering the unmanned capabilities of the world’s most powerful military.
US Secretary of Defence Pete Hegseth subsequently called for more proactive action in July, urging the US military to aggressively procure small UAVs and related components, with the goal of widespread deployment of these systems by late next year.

data.title: Russia ‘strikes Ukraine before Christmas’ with over 650 drones, dozens of missiles
data.description:
Russia fired more than 650 drones and three dozen missiles at Ukraine in a large-scale attack that began during the night and stretched into daylight hours Tuesday, officials said. At least three people were killed, including a 4-year-old child.
The barrage struck homes and the power grid in 13 regions of Ukraine, causing widespread outages in bitter temperatures, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said, a day after he described recent progress on finding a peace deal “quite solid”.
The attack “is an extremely clear signal of Russian priorities” Zelensky said on the Telegram messaging app.
“A strike before Christmas, when people want to be with their families, at home, in safety. A strike, in fact, in the midst of negotiations that are being conducted to end this war. Putin cannot accept the fact that we must stop killing.”


Ukrainian and European officials have complained that Russian President Vladimir Putin is not sincerely engaging with US-led peace efforts.
US President Donald Trump has for months been pressing for a peace agreement, but the negotiations have become entangled in the very different demands from Moscow and Kyiv.
US envoy Steve Witkoff said on Sunday he held “productive and constructive” talks in Florida with Ukrainian and European representatives. Trump was less effusive on Monday, saying, “The talks are going along.”
Initial reports from Ukrainian emergency services said the 4-year-old child died in Ukraine’s northwestern Zhytomyr region, while a drone killed a woman in the Kyiv region, and another civilian death was recorded in the western Khmelnytskyi region, according to Volodymyr Zelensky.

Authorities in the western regions of Rivne, Ternopil and Lviv, as well as the northern Sumy region, reported damage to energy infrastructure or power outages after the attack.
In the southern Odesa region, Russia struck energy, port, transport, industrial and residential infrastructure, according to regional head Oleh Kiper.
A merchant ship and over 120 homes were damaged, he said.
Russia’s latest attacks once again focused primarily on Ukraine’s energy infrastructure.
The situation for many residents has deteriorated due to freezing temperatures, with electricity and heating disruptions compounding existing hardships.
Moscow has intensified strikes during the winter months in an apparent effort to increase pressure on Ukraine to accept Russian terms in ongoing talks aimed at ending the war.
Additional reporting by dpa

data.title: Hong Kong tram driver gets 4 weeks in jail for crash that killed girl, 3
data.description:
A Hong Kong court handed down a four-week jail sentence to a tram driver earlier convicted for careless driving that killed a three-year-old girl and injured three others, with the punishment significantly reduced due to the accident being deemed “inevitable”.
Deputy Magistrate Chung Wing-sze of Eastern Court on Tuesday cited a probation officer’s report that 50-year-old Li Yaodong was genuinely remorseful and had accepted legal responsibility.
Chung also said the defendant’s colleagues had petitioned for leniency, praising him as “dedicated to his work” and pointing to him twice winning the “best driver” award during his five years of service.
The accident took place outside Kennedy Town Swimming Pool on the morning of August 15, 2024. Li’s tram struck the girl, her six-year-old sister, their grandfather and the family’s helper as they were crossing Des Voeux Road West.
The girl was found unconscious under the tram and later pronounced dead in hospital.
Evidence showed that the grandfather carried the girl with one arm and held the hand of her sister as they waited at a traffic island. Security footage indicated that they took a step forward despite a red light being on.
While Li said he had already stopped the vehicle after realising that the tram had struck the pedestrians, Chung found the driver did not pay enough attention to road conditions on a rainy day and did not brake in time.
“But considering the uniqueness of each case, a tram cannot swerve, and it takes time to brake the vehicle. Therefore, the collision was inevitable,” she said. “These factors will significantly reduce the sentence.”
The magistrate said she had taken into consideration mitigation letters from the defendant’s colleagues at Hong Kong Tramways, where he worked from 2019 to 2024 after moving to the city from mainland China.
Chung added that Li’s colleagues had signed a petition letter to plead for a lenient sentence.
The court earlier heard from an expert’s report that the collision occurred in a split second, giving the defendant only 0.9 seconds to react.
Driving records showed that the tram continued moving for another 12.7 metres (41.7 feet) after the impact, indicating that Li failed to stop in time.
The report concluded that the accident was inevitable as the driver “may or may not have been able” to brake in time.
Chung sentenced Li to four weeks in prison after taking into consideration the case’s mitigating factors.
Defence lawyer Leo Yu indicated that Li would appeal and applied for the defendant’s bail.
The court granted Li a HK$10,000 (US$1,285) bail but barred him from leaving the city during the proceedings and told him to report regularly to his local police station.
Chung also ordered that the defendant be released pending appeal proceedings, noting that he had already been remanded in custody for two weeks.
